content:
In our last part we left with a question. Each tribal leader brought a silver plate of We have now studied the silver plate at 130 shekels, pointing to the openly displayed ransom price of Christ and the new beginning born out of fallen man’s condition. We have studied the silver bowl at 70 shekels, pointing to the depth of Christ’s voluntary sacrifice and the full reach of His redemption to all 70 nations. Today we move from silver to gold. Along with the two silver vessels, each tribal leader brought one golden vessel; a small golden spoon or cup, weighing 10 shekels, filled with pure incense. This single gold item carried a fragrance. And as we will see, everything about this vessel; its material, its shape, its content, and its weight is speaking.

The Hebrew word used here for the golden vessel is kaph. It literally means the palm of a hand; the hollow of an open palm cupped to receive and carry something precious. It is the same word used when we read of someone holding something gently in the palm of their hands. The golden spoon of the offering was not a large vessel. It was the size of a hand. It was personal, close, and carried at body level; not placed on a cart, not set on a platform, but held in the palm of the one who brought it.

The fact that this vessel was made of gold is the first message. Throughout our studies we have seen that gold in Scripture speaks of the divine character; purified faith without mixture. The silver vessels spoke of redemption. But the gold vessel speaks of something that belongs to the divine nature itself.
What was in that golden palm? Not flour. Not oil. Pure incense. The silver carried what feeds. The gold carried what rises. And what rises before God as incense, Scripture is very clear about.

Psalm 141:2 gives us David’s prayer: “Let my prayer be set before You as incense, the lifting up of my hands as the evening sacrifice.” David understood that his prayer was not merely words going into the air. It was fragrance rising before the face of God. Revelation 5:8 shows us the heavenly reality directly: the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders each held golden bowls full of incense, and then John tells us exactly what the incense was: “which are the prayers of the saints.” Not golden bowls of literal incense. Golden bowls of prayers. The incense and the prayer are one thing in the sight of God.
And Revelation 8:3-4 takes this further: an angel stood before the golden altar holding a golden censer, and much incense was given to him to offer with the prayers of all the saints before the throne, and the smoke of the incense rose before God together with the prayers.

So the golden spoon filled with incense that each tribal leader brought to the altar of dedication was a vessel of prayer. Twelve leaders. Twelve golden palms. Twelve vessels of prayer laid before Yahweh on twelve consecutive days. Each one carrying the fragrance of a people coming before God with uplifted hands, with surrendered hearts, with the worship of an entire tribe represented in one small golden cup. And now we must ask: if the twelve golden spoons represent the twelve tribes and their prayer before God, do the twelve also point to something greater in the new covenant? Yes. The twelve golden spoons can be understood as the twelve foundation stones; the twelve apostles of the Lamb on whose testimony the new covenant assembly is built (Revelation 21:14). The twelve foundations carry the prayers of the whole people of God. The twelve apostolic testimonies are the golden palms through which the worship and intercession of all the nations is lifted before the face of Yahweh.

There is one more thing to observe about the golden spoon before we come to its weight. The spoon did not produce the incense. It carried it. The incense was placed in the spoon by the priest. The spoon’s only work was to hold what it had been given and bring it to the place of offering. This is the picture of every believer’s life of prayer and worship. We do not produce the fragrance by our own natural ability. We are the vessel. The fragrance is what Yahweh places within us.

Paul wrote in 2 Corinthians 2:14-15: “Thanks be to God, who in Christ always leads us in triumphal procession, and through us spreads the fragrance of the knowledge of Him everywhere. For we are the aroma of Christ to God among those who are being saved and among those who are perishing.” We are the aroma of Christ. Not the producers of the aroma, but the carriers. The incense in the spoon was not made by the spoon. The fragrance of Christ in our lives is not made by us. It is placed in us by Christ, through the Spirit, and we carry it wherever we go. Some receive it as the smell of life. Others receive it as the smell of death (2 Corinthians 2:16). But the carrier simply carries. The golden spoon simply holds what it has been filled with and walks to the altar.

The golden spoon weighed 10 shekels. We have seen 130, we have seen 70, we have seen how deeply numbers speak in this offering. So what does 10 speak here? And when we multiply 10 by 12, we get 120 shekels of gold in total. What does 120 mean? That number appears at three of the most significant transition points in all of Scripture, and together they form a picture that is breathtaking in its precision. We will study this tomorrow.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, you are the God who receives the fragrance of prayer. You did not ask us to come before You with empty hands. You filled the golden spoon with incense and said: carry this to My altar. We thank You that our prayers are not just words sent into an empty sky, but fragrance rising before Your face, held in the golden palms of the intercession of Christ. We thank You that we are not asked to produce the fragrance ourselves; that You place the incense in us and help us produce the fragrance. Forgive us for the days when the golden spoon was empty; when we came before You without prayer, without worship, without the incense of a heart turned toward You. Fill us again today. Make us carriers of the fragrance of Christ; to those who are searching for the smell of life, and even to those who are not yet searching. In Christ’s name, amen.
